# Optional Dependencies


::: {.scenario}
*Or what to Do When “Optional” Dependencies Don’t Show Up*

This scenario demonstrates `<optional>true</optional>` flag.



```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root["Project Root"] --> LibA["lib-a:1.0.0 (optional)"]
```
:::


```{.xml include="pom.xml" snippet="s8-deps"}
```

#### Reader Expectation
Optional dependencies are omitted from a consumer's transitive graph by default.

#### Why This Happens
The optional flag prevents consumer classpaths from being "polluted" with libraries required only for specific, non-universal features.

#### How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
If a consumer *does* need the optional feature, they must explicitly declare the dependency themselves.

#### Safer Ways to Take Control
Document optional dependencies clearly so consumers know what to add.

#### Deterministic Checks

**Fast check (Maven):**

When project `B` depends on project `A` (which has an optional dependency on `lib-a`), run this in project `B`:
```bash
mvn dependency:tree
```

**What to look for:**
`lib-a` will be absent from the dependency tree of project `B`.

**Conclusion:**
Optional dependencies are not transitive. Consumers must explicitly declare them if needed.

#### How This Scales in Real Systems
Used heavily by large frameworks (Spring, Hibernate) to support many integrations without forcing all of them on everyone.

#### Key Takeaway
Optional dependencies shift the burden of declaration to the consumer.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 7: Scopes](../scenario-7-scopes/guide.qmd) - `provided` scope is similar but semantically different.
